Math4201Topology I (Lecture 35)

Math4201 Topology I (Lecture 35)

Countability axioms

Kolmogorov classification

Consider the topological space XXX.

XXX is T0T_0T0​ means for every pair of points x,y∈Xx,y\in Xx,y∈X, x≠yx\neq yx=y, there is one of xxx and yyy is in an open set UUU containing xxx but not yyy.

XXX is T1T_1T1​ means for every pair of points x,y∈Xx,y\in Xx,y∈X, x≠yx\neq yx=y, each of them have a open set UUU and VVV such that x∈Ux\in Ux∈U and y∈Vy\in Vy∈V and x∉Vx\notin Vx∈/V and y∉Uy\notin Uy∈/U. (singleton sets are closed)

XXX is T2T_2T2​ means for every pair of points x,y∈Xx,y\in Xx,y∈X, x≠yx\neq yx=y, there exists disjoint open sets UUU and VVV such that x∈Ux\in Ux∈U and y∈Vy\in Vy∈V. (Hausdorff)

XXX is T3T_3T3​ means that XXX is regular: for any x∈Xx\in Xx∈X and any close set A⊆XA\subseteq XA⊆X such that x∉Ax\notin Ax∈/A, there are disjoint open sets U,VU,VU,V such that x∈Ux\in Ux∈U and A⊆VA\subseteq VA⊆V.

XXX is T4T_4T4​ means that XXX is normal: for any disjoint closed sets, A,B⊆XA,B\subseteq XA,B⊆X, there are disjoint open sets U,VU,VU,V such that A⊆UA\subseteq UA⊆U and B⊆VB\subseteq VB⊆V.

Example

Let Rℓ\mathbb{R}_{\ell}Rℓ​ with lower limit topology.

Rℓ\mathbb{R}_{\ell}Rℓ​ is normal since for any disjoint closed sets, A,B⊆RℓA,B\subseteq \mathbb{R}_{\ell}A,B⊆Rℓ​, x∈Ax\in Ax∈A and BBB is closed and doesn’t contain xxx. Then there exists ϵx>0\epsilon_x>0ϵx​>0 such that [x,x+ϵx)⊆A[x,x+\epsilon_x)\subseteq A[x,x+ϵx​)⊆A and does not intersect BBB.

Therefore, there exists δy>0\delta_y>0δy​>0 such that [y,y+δy)⊆B[y,y+\delta_y)\subseteq B[y,y+δy​)⊆B and does not intersect AAA.

Let U=⋃x∈A[x,x+ϵx)U=\bigcup_{x\in A}[x,x+\epsilon_x)U=⋃x∈A​[x,x+ϵx​) is open and contains AAA.

V=⋃y∈B[y,y+δy)V=\bigcup_{y\in B}[y,y+\delta_y)V=⋃y∈B​[y,y+δy​) is open and contains BBB.

We show that UUU and VVV are disjoint.

If U∩V≠∅U\cap V\neq \emptysetU∩V=∅, then there exists x∈Ax\in Ax∈A and Y∈BY\in BY∈B such that [x,x+ϵx)∩[y,y+δy)≠∅[x,x+\epsilon_x)\cap [y,y+\delta_y)\neq \emptyset[x,x+ϵx​)∩[y,y+δy​)=∅.

This is a contradiction since [x,x+ϵx)⊆A[x,x+\epsilon_x)\subseteq A[x,x+ϵx​)⊆A and [y,y+δy)⊆B[y,y+\delta_y)\subseteq B[y,y+δy​)⊆B.

Theorem Every metric space is normal

Use the similar proof above.

Proof

Let A,B⊆XA,B\subseteq XA,B⊆X be closed.

Since BBB is closed, for any x∈Ax\in Ax∈A, there exists ϵx>0\epsilon_x>0ϵx​>0 such that Bϵx(x)⊆BB_{\epsilon_x}(x)\subseteq BBϵx​​(x)⊆B.

Since AAA is closed, for any y∈By\in By∈B, there exists δy>0\delta_y>0δy​>0 such that Aδy(y)⊆AA_{\delta_y}(y)\subseteq AAδy​​(y)⊆A.

Let U=⋃x∈ABϵx/2(x)U=\bigcup_{x\in A}B_{\epsilon_x/2}(x)U=⋃x∈A​Bϵx​/2​(x) and V=⋃y∈BBδy/2(y)V=\bigcup_{y\in B}B_{\delta_y/2}(y)V=⋃y∈B​Bδy​/2​(y).

We show that UUU and VVV are disjoint.

If U∩V≠∅U\cap V\neq \emptysetU∩V=∅, then there exists x∈Ax\in Ax∈A and Y∈BY\in BY∈B such that Bϵx/2(x)∩Bδy/2(y)≠∅B_{\epsilon_x/2}(x)\cap B_{\delta_y/2}(y)\neq \emptysetBϵx​/2​(x)∩Bδy​/2​(y)=∅.

Consider z∈Bϵx/2(x)∩Bδy/2(y)z\in B_{\epsilon_x/2}(x)\cap B_{\delta_y/2}(y)z∈Bϵx​/2​(x)∩Bδy​/2​(y). Then d(x,z)<ϵx/2d(x,z)<\epsilon_x/2d(x,z)<ϵx​/2 and d(y,z)<δy/2d(y,z)<\delta_y/2d(y,z)<δy​/2. Therefore d(x,y)≤d(x,z)+d(z,y)<ϵx/2+δy/2d(x,y)\leq d(x,z)+d(z,y)<\epsilon_x/2+\delta_y/2d(x,y)≤d(x,z)+d(z,y)<ϵx​/2+δy​/2.

If δy<ϵx\delta_y<\epsilon_xδy​<ϵx​, then d(x,y)<δy/2+δy/2=δyd(x,y)<\delta_y/2+\delta_y/2=\delta_yd(x,y)<δy​/2+δy​/2=δy​. Therefore x∈Bδy(y)⊆Ax\in B_{\delta_y}(y)\subseteq Ax∈Bδy​​(y)⊆A. This is a contradiction since U∩B=∅U\cap B=\emptysetU∩B=∅.

If ϵx<δy\epsilon_x<\delta_yϵx​<δy​, then d(x,y)<ϵx/2+ϵx/2=ϵxd(x,y)<\epsilon_x/2+\epsilon_x/2=\epsilon_xd(x,y)<ϵx​/2+ϵx​/2=ϵx​. Therefore y∈Bϵx(x)⊆By\in B_{\epsilon_x}(x)\subseteq By∈Bϵx​​(x)⊆B. This is a contradiction since V∩A=∅V\cap A=\emptysetV∩A=∅.

Therefore, UUU and VVV are disjoint.

Lemma fo regular topological space

XXX is regular topological space if and only if for any x∈Xx\in Xx∈X and any open neighborhood UUU of xxx, there is open neighborhood VVV of xxx such that V‾⊆U\overline{V}\subseteq UV⊆U.

Lemma of normal topological space

XXX is a normal topological space if and only if for any A⊆XA\subseteq XA⊆X closed and any open neighborhood UUU of AAA, there is open neighborhood VVV of AAA such that V‾⊆U\overline{V}\subseteq UV⊆U.

Proof

  ⟹  \implies⟹

Let AAA and UUU are given as in the statement.

So AAA and (X−U)(X-U)(X−U) are disjoint closed.

Since XXX is normal and A⊆V⊆XA\subseteq V\subseteq XA⊆V⊆X and V∩W=∅V\cap W=\emptysetV∩W=∅. X−U⊆W⊆XX-U\subseteq W\subseteq XX−U⊆W⊆X. where WWW is open in XXX.

And V‾⊆(X−W)⊆U\overline{V}\subseteq (X-W)\subseteq UV⊆(X−W)⊆U.

And A⊆VA\subseteq VA⊆V.

The proof of reverse direction is similar.

Let A,BA,BA,B be disjoint and closed.

Then A⊆U≔X−B⊆XA\subseteq U\coloneqq X-B\subseteq XA⊆U:=X−B⊆X and X−BX-BX−B is open in XXX.

Apply the assumption to find A⊆V⊆XA\subseteq V\subseteq XA⊆V⊆X and VVV is open in XXX and V‾⊆U≔X−B\overline{V}\subseteq U\coloneqq X-BV⊆U:=X−B.

Proposition of regular and Hausdorff on subspaces

  1. If XXX is a regular topological space, and YYY is a subspace. Then YYY with induced topology is regular. (same holds for Hausdorff)
  2. If {Xα}\{X_\alpha\}{Xα​} is a collection of regular topological spaces, then their product with the product topology is regular. (same holds for Hausdorff)
Caution

The above does not hold for normal.

Recall that Rℓ\mathbb{R}_{\ell}Rℓ​ with lower limit topology is normal. But Rℓ×Rℓ\mathbb{R}_{\ell}\times \mathbb{R}_{\ell}Rℓ​×Rℓ​ with product topology is not normal.

Proof that Sorgenfrey plane is not normal

The goal of this problem is to show that Rℓ2\mathbb{R}^2_\ellRℓ2​ (the Sorgenfrey plane) is not normal. Recall that Rℓ\mathbb{R}_\ellRℓ​ is the real line with the lower limit topology, and Rℓ2\mathbb{R}_\ell^2Rℓ2​ is equipped with the product topology. Consider the subset

L={ (x,−x)∣x∈Rℓ }⊂Rℓ2.L = \{\, (x,-x) \mid x\in \mathbb{R}_\ell \,\} \subset \mathbb{R}^2_\ell.L={(x,−x)∣x∈Rℓ​}⊂Rℓ2​.

Let A⊂LA\subset LA⊂L be the set points of the form (x,−x)(x,-x)(x,−x) such that xxx is rational and B⊂LB\subset LB⊂L be the set points of the form (x,−x)(x,-x)(x,−x) such that xxx is irrational.

  1. Show that the subspace topology on LLL is the discrete topology. Conclude that AAA and BBB are closed subspaces of Rℓ2\mathbb{R}_\ell^2Rℓ2​

Proof

First we show that $L$ is closed.

Consider x=(a,b)∈Rℓ2−Lx=(a,b)\in\mathbb{R}^2_\ell-Lx=(a,b)∈Rℓ2​−L, by definition a≠−ba\neq -ba=−b.

If a>−ba>-ba>−b, then there exists open neighborhood Ux=[min⁡{a,b}2,a+1)×[min⁡{a,b}2,b+1)U_x=[\frac{\min\{a,b\}}{2},a+1)\times[\frac{\min\{a,b\}}{2},b+1)Ux​=[2min{a,b}​,a+1)×[2min{a,b}​,b+1) that is disjoint from LLL (no points of form (x,−x)(x,-x)(x,−x) in our rectangle), therefore x∈Uxx\in U_xx∈Ux​.

If a<−ba<-ba<−b, then there exists open neighborhood Ux=[a,a+min⁡{a,b}2)×[b,b+min⁡{a,b}2)U_x=[a,a+\frac{\min\{a,b\}}{2})\times[b,b+\frac{\min\{a,b\}}{2})Ux​=[a,a+2min{a,b}​)×[b,b+2min{a,b}​) that is disjoint from LLL, therefore x∈Uxx\in U_xx∈Ux​.

Therefore, Rℓ2−L=⋃x∈Rℓ2−LUx\mathbb{R}^2_\ell-L=\bigcup_{x\in \mathbb{R}_\ell^2-L} U_xRℓ2​−L=⋃x∈Rℓ2​−L​Ux​ is open in Rℓ2\mathbb{R}^2_\ellRℓ2​.

So LLL is closed in Rℓ2\mathbb{R}^2_\ellRℓ2​.

To show LLL with subspace topology on Rℓ2\mathbb{R}^2_\ellRℓ2​ is discrete topology, we need to show that every singleton of LLL is open in LLL.

For each {(x,−x)}∈L\{(x,-x)\}\in L{(x,−x)}∈L, [x,x+1)×[−x,−x+1)[x,x+1)\times [-x,-x+1)[x,x+1)×[−x,−x+1) is open in Rℓ2\mathbb{R}_\ell^2Rℓ2​ and {(x,−x)}=([x,x+1)×[−x,−x+1))∩L\{(x,-x)\}=([x,x+1)\times [-x,-x+1))\cap L{(x,−x)}=([x,x+1)×[−x,−x+1))∩L, therefore {(x,−x)}\{(x,-x)\}{(x,−x)} is open in LLL.

Since A,BA,BA,B are disjoint and A∪B=LA\cup B=LA∪B=L, therefore A=L−BA=L-BA=L−B and B=L−AB=L-AB=L−A, by definition of discrete topology, A,BA,BA,B are both open therefore the complement of A,BA,BA,B are closed. So A,BA,BA,B are closed in LLL.

since LLL is closed in Rℓ2\mathbb{R}^2_\ellRℓ2​, by \textbf{Lemma \ref{closed_set_close_subspace_close}}, A,BA,BA,B is also closed in Rℓ2\mathbb{R}_\ell^2Rℓ2​. Therefore A,BA,BA,B are closed subspace of Rℓ2\mathbb{R}_\ell^2Rℓ2​.

  1. Let VVV be an open set of Rℓ2\mathbb{R}^2_\ellRℓ2​ containing BBB. Let KnK_nKn​ consist of all irrational numbers x∈[0,1]x\in [0,1]x∈[0,1] such that [x,x+1/n)×[−x,−x+1/n)[x, x+1/n) \times [-x, -x+1/n)[x,x+1/n)×[−x,−x+1/n) is contained in VVV. Show that [0,1][0,1][0,1] is the union of the sets KnK_nKn​ and countably many one-point sets.

Proof

Since $B$ is open in $L$, for each $b\in B$, by definition of basis in $\mathbb{R}_\ell^2$, and $B$ is open, there exists $b\in ([b,b+\epsilon)\times [-b,-b+\delta))\cap L\subseteq V$ and $0<\epsilon,\delta$, so there exists $n_b$ such that $\frac{1}{n_b}<\min\{\epsilon,\delta\}$ such that $b\in ([b,b+\frac{1}{n_b})\times [-b,-b+\frac{1}{n_b}))\cap L\subseteq V$.

Therefore ⋃n=1∞Kn\bigcup_{n=1}^\infty K_n⋃n=1∞​Kn​ covers irrational points in [0,1][0,1][0,1]

Note that B=L−AB=L-AB=L−A where AAA is rational points therefore countable.

So [0,1][0,1][0,1] is the union of the sets KnK_nKn​ and countably many one-point sets.

  1. Use Problem 5-3 to show that some set Kn‾\overline{K_n}Kn​​ contains an open interval (a,b)(a,b)(a,b) of R\mathbb{R}R. (You don’t need to prove Problem 5.3, if it is not your choice of #5.)

Lemma

Let XXX be a compact Hausdorff space; let {An}\{A_n\}{An​} be a countable collection of closed sets of XXX. If each sets AnA_nAn​ has empty interior in XXX, then the union ⋃n=1∞An\bigcup_{n=1}^\infty A_n⋃n=1∞​An​ has empty interior in XXX.

Proof

We proceed by contradiction, note that $[0,1]$ is a compact Hausdorff space since it's closed and bounded.

And {Kn‾}n=0∞\{\overline{K_n}\}_{n=0}^\infty{Kn​​}n=0∞​ is a countable collection of closed sets of [0,1][0,1][0,1].

Suppose for the sake of contradiction, Kn‾\overline{K_n}Kn​​ has empty interior in XXX for all n∈Nn\in \mathbb{N}n∈N, by \textbf{Lemma \ref{countable_closed_sets_empty_interior}}, then ⋃n=1∞Kn‾\bigcup_{n=1}^\infty \overline{K_n}⋃n=1∞​Kn​​ has empty interior in [0,1][0,1][0,1], where \Q∩[0,1]\Q\cap[0,1]\Q∩[0,1] are countably union of singletons, therefore has empty interior in [0,1][0,1][0,1].

Therefore ⋃n=1∞Kn‾\bigcup_{n=1}^\infty \overline{K_n}⋃n=1∞​Kn​​ has empty interior in [0,1][0,1][0,1], since ⋃n=1∞Kn⊆⋃n=1∞Kn‾\bigcup_{n=1}^\infty K_n\subseteq \bigcup_{n=1}^\infty \overline{K_n}⋃n=1∞​Kn​⊆⋃n=1∞​Kn​​, ⋃n=1∞Kn\bigcup_{n=1}^\infty K_n⋃n=1∞​Kn​ also has empty interior in [0,1][0,1][0,1] by definition of subspace of [0,1][0,1][0,1], therefore ⋃n=1∞Kn∪(\Q∩[0,1])\bigcup_{n=1}^\infty K_n\cup (\Q\cap[0,1])⋃n=1∞​Kn​∪(\Q∩[0,1]) has empty interior in [0,1][0,1][0,1]. This contradicts that ⋃n=1∞Kn∪(\Q∩[0,1])\bigcup_{n=1}^\infty K_n\cup (\Q\cap[0,1])⋃n=1∞​Kn​∪(\Q∩[0,1]) covers [0,1][0,1][0,1] and should at least have interior (0.1,0.9)(0.1,0.9)(0.1,0.9).

  1. Show that VVV contains the open parallelogram consisting of all points of the form
x×(−x+ϵ) for which a<x<b and 0<ϵ<1n.x\times (-x+\epsilon)\quad\text{ for which }\quad a<x<b\text{ and }0<\epsilon<\frac{1}{n}.x×(−x+ϵ) for which a<x<b and 0<ϵ<n1​.

Proof

Since $V$ is open, by previous problem we know that there exists $n$ such that $\overline{K_n}$ contains the open interval $(a,b)$.

If x∈Knx\in K_nx∈Kn​, ∀a<x<b\forall a<x<b∀a<x<b, by definition of KnK_nKn​ [x,x+1n)×[−x,−x+1n)⊆V[x,x+\frac{1}{n})\times [-x,-x+\frac{1}{n})\subseteq V[x,x+n1​)×[−x,−x+n1​)⊆V.

If xxx is a limit point of KnK_nKn​, since VVV is open, there exists 0<ϵ<1n0<\epsilon<\frac{1}{n}0<ϵ<n1​ such that [x,x+ϵ)×[−x,−x+ϵ)⊆V[x,x+\epsilon)\times [-x,-x+\epsilon)\subseteq V[x,x+ϵ)×[−x,−x+ϵ)⊆V.

This gives our desired open parallelogram.

  1. Show that if qqq is a rational number with a<q<ba<q<ba<q<b, then the point q×(−q)q\times (-q)q×(−q) of Rℓ2\mathbb{R}_\ell^2Rℓ2​ is a limit point of VVV. Conclude that there are no disjoint open neighborhoods UUU of AAA and VVV of BBB.

Proof

Consider all the open neighborhood of $q\times (-q)$ in $\mathbb{R}_\ell^2$, for all $\delta>0$, $[q,q+\delta)\times (-q,-q+\delta)$ will intersect with some $x\times [-x,-x+\epsilon)\subseteq V$ such that $0<\epsilon<\frac{1}{n}<\delta$.

Therefore, any open set containing q×(−q)∈Aq\times (-q)\in Aq×(−q)∈A will intersect with VVV, it is impossible to build disjoint open neighborhoods UUU of AAA and VVV of BBB.

This shows that Rℓ\mathbb{R}_{\ell}Rℓ​ is not metrizable. Otherwise Rℓ×Rℓ\mathbb{R}_{\ell}\times \mathbb{R}_{\ell}Rℓ​×Rℓ​ would be metrizable. Which could implies that Rℓ\mathbb{R}_{\ell}Rℓ​ is normal.

Theorem of metrizability (Urysohn metirzation theorem)

If XXX is normal and second countable, then XXX is metrizable.

Note
  • Every metrizable topological space is normal.
  • Every metrizable space is first countable.
  • But there are some metrizable space that is not second countable.

Note that if XXX is normal and first countable, then it is not necessarily metrizable. (Example Rℓ\mathbb{R}_{\ell}Rℓ​)
